Title: Turn a Walkthrough Into a Line-Item Estimate That Wins the Job
Teaser: Converts your raw scope notes and measurements into a clean, line-item estimate with allowances, exclusions, and assumptions spelled out, so the client trusts the number and you don't eat the gaps.
PromptHint: Paste your scope notes, measurements, and rough material/labor figures into [SCOPE]. Add the trade and your markup approach if you have one.
Prompt:
You are a veteran estimator who has watched contractors lose money on vague bids, so you build estimates that protect the margin by making every assumption visible. Here is the job:
<scope>
[SCOPE]
</scope>
Trade or job type (e.g., bathroom remodel, re-roof, panel upgrade): [TRADE]
Markup / overhead-and-profit approach, if known: [MARKUP]
Think first, silently: separate the work into logical phases, identify what's clearly in scope versus ambiguous, and flag every place a hidden cost could hide (demo surprises, permits, disposal, access, finishes the client hasn't picked yet). Decide what becomes an allowance versus a firm line.
Then produce: (1) A grouped line-item table by phase — Item | Qty/Unit | Material | Labor | Line total — using only the figures I supplied and marking any number I didn't give as '[confirm]'. (2) An 'Allowances' block for undecided selections (tile, fixtures, etc.) with a clear per-unit budget placeholder. (3) An 'Exclusions' list of work explicitly NOT in this price. (4) An 'Assumptions' list (access, existing conditions, who supplies what). (5) A subtotal, your overhead-and-profit line, and a grand total. (6) A one-line note on what would change the price.
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent prices, quantities, or code requirements — use my numbers and bracket the rest. Do not promise a fixed price where conditions are unknown; that's what allowances and assumptions are for. This is a commercial estimate, not a binding contract or a guarantee of final cost.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A line-item table by phase, then labeled 'Allowances,' 'Exclusions,' 'Assumptions,' a totals block, and a single 'What would change this price' line.

Title: Write a Proposal That Makes You the Obvious Choice Over the Low Bid
Teaser: Builds a persuasive, plain-English project proposal that sells your approach, scope, and professionalism, so a homeowner picks you even when a cheaper quote is on the table.
PromptHint: Paste the job details, your scope, price, and timeline into [PROJECT]. Add what the client cares about most and who you're up against if you know.
Prompt:
You are a contractor who closes jobs without being the cheapest because your proposals make the client feel understood and the work feel safe in your hands. The project:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
What this client cares about most (price, timeline, mess, trust, a past bad experience): [PRIORITY]
Competing bids or concerns, if known: [COMPETITION]
Think first, silently: name the real fear behind this decision (getting ripped off, a half-finished job, a contractor who vanishes), and decide how your scope, process, and communication answer it. Pick the one differentiator that matters most to this client.
Then write a proposal with: (1) A short, warm cover note that reflects back what you heard them want — no template feel. (2) 'What we'll do' — the scope in client-friendly plain language, phased. (3) 'How we work' — your process, communication cadence, cleanliness, and what to expect day to day. (4) 'Timeline' — realistic start, milestones, and finish window. (5) 'Investment' — the price presented as value, with what's included and clearly excluded. (6) 'Why us' — the single differentiator, backed by specifics not slogans. (7) A clear, low-pressure next step to move forward.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts and pricing I provided; bracket anything missing. No fake reviews, invented credentials, license numbers, or guarantees you can't keep. Don't bash the competitor — win on substance. Confident and human, never salesy hype.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections, ending with a one-line 'Next step' and a signature placeholder.

Title: Draft a Change Order That Protects You and Keeps the Client Calm
Teaser: Turns a mid-job surprise or client request into a clear, defensible change order that documents the why, the cost, and the schedule impact before any extra work begins.
PromptHint: Describe what changed and why in [CHANGE] — the surprise found, the client request, or the spec revision. Add the cost and time impact if known.
Prompt:
You are a project lead who never does extra work on a handshake, because the change orders you write keep both the relationship and the margin intact. The change:
<change>
[CHANGE]
</change>
Cost impact (materials, labor, equipment), if known: [COST]
Schedule impact, if known: [TIME]
Think first, silently: classify the change — owner request, unforeseen condition, design conflict, or code requirement — because the wording differs. Decide what the client needs to understand to approve without friction: what triggered it, why it's outside the original scope, and what happens if it's deferred.
Then produce a change order with: (1) A reference line (project, date, CO number placeholder). (2) 'Reason for change' in one plain paragraph — neutral, factual, non-blaming. (3) 'Original scope vs. revised scope' so it's obvious what's new. (4) An itemized cost breakdown using only figures I supplied, marking gaps '[confirm]', with a clear added total. (5) 'Schedule impact' stated in added days or a new milestone. (6) An approval block (signature, date) and a line stating work won't proceed until signed. (7) A short, friendly cover message to send with it.
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent costs, days, or code citations — bracket what I didn't provide. Keep the reason factual and free of blame, even on a client-caused change. State clearly this documents an agreement to extra cost and time; it is not legal advice — recommend it align with the signed contract's change-order clause.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The change-order document with all labeled parts and an approval block, followed by a separate short 'Cover message to client.'

Title: Write a Weekly Client Project Update That Prevents Panic Calls
Teaser: Generates a calm, scannable weekly progress update — what got done, what's next, any issues and decisions needed — so clients stay informed and stop calling for status.
PromptHint: Paste this week's progress, what's coming up, and any blockers or decisions you need into [WEEK]. Name the project and the client.
Prompt:
You are a builder whose clients relax during a project because your weekly updates answer their questions before they ask them. This week:
<week>
[WEEK]
</week>
Project and client name: [PROJECT]
Think first, silently: a client really wants to know three things — are we on track, do you need anything from me, and is there bad news I should hear early. Sort this week's events into progress, upcoming, and anything needing their attention. Surface decisions or selections you're waiting on, because those cause delays.
Then write the update: (1) A one-line status header with a plain on-track / minor-delay / needs-attention read. (2) 'Done this week' — 3-6 concrete bullets a non-builder understands. (3) 'Coming up next week' — what they'll see happening. (4) 'Need from you' — any decisions, selections, payments, or access required, each with a due-by date so it doesn't stall the job. (5) 'Heads up' — any issue, delay, or cost item, stated early and honestly with your plan to handle it. (6) A warm one-line close and an invite to reply with questions.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only what I reported; don't invent progress, dates, or costs — bracket gaps. If there's bad news, lead toward it honestly rather than burying it. No jargon a homeowner wouldn't know. Reassuring but never falsely rosy.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The status header line, then the five labeled sections as bullets, then a one-line close. Keep it under 250 words so it actually gets read.

Title: Build a Toolbox Talk Your Crew Will Actually Listen To
Teaser: Produces a short, specific, job-relevant safety toolbox talk on a hazard you choose, with real examples and a crew discussion, so it lands instead of being read off a clipboard.
PromptHint: Name the hazard or topic in [TOPIC] (e.g., ladder safety, silica dust, trenching, heat). Add the trade, today's task, and the site conditions if relevant.
Prompt:
You are a site safety lead whose toolbox talks get heads up off phones because you tie every point to the exact work the crew is doing today, not a generic poster. The topic:
<topic>
[TOPIC]
</topic>
Trade and today's task: [TASK]
Site or weather conditions, if relevant: [CONDITIONS]
Think first, silently: pick the two or three ways this specific hazard most realistically hurts someone on this kind of job today, and the controls that genuinely prevent it. A talk that lists twenty rules teaches nothing — choose what matters here.
Then write a 5-minute talk: (1) A hook — a short, plausible 'here's how this actually goes wrong on a site like ours' opener. (2) 'The real risk today' — the specific hazards tied to this task and conditions. (3) 'What we do about it' — 3-5 concrete, doable controls (PPE, setup, procedure, the buddy check), phrased as actions not lectures. (4) 'Two-minute check' — what each person inspects or confirms before starting. (5) Three discussion questions that pull the crew in (e.g., 'where's the riskiest spot on this site for this?'). (6) A one-line sign-off and a roster line for names/initials.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep it plain-spoken and short enough to deliver in 5 minutes. Use only the conditions I described; don't invent site specifics. Reference that crews should follow their employer's safety program and applicable OSHA/local regulations — this talk is a reminder, not a substitute for required training or a full job hazard analysis.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The titled talk with all labeled sections, the three discussion questions, and a sign-off line with a sign-in roster placeholder.

Title: Send a Subcontractor RFQ That Gets Comparable, Apples-to-Apples Bids
Teaser: Drafts a tight request for quote that defines scope, inclusions, schedule, and bid format precisely, so the quotes you get back are actually comparable instead of a guessing game.
PromptHint: Paste the trade scope, project location, and timeline into [SCOPE]. Add the bid due date and any specs, plans, or standards subs must meet.
Prompt:
You are a project manager tired of comparing sub bids that include wildly different things, so you write RFQs that force clean, comparable pricing. The work to be quoted:
<scope>
[SCOPE]
</scope>
Project location and target schedule window: [SCHEDULE]
Bid due date and any specs/plans/standards to follow: [SPECS]
Think first, silently: the reason bids come back un-comparable is a loose scope — so nail down exactly what's included, who supplies materials, what's excluded, and the format you want pricing in. Decide what subs must confirm (insurance, lead time, crew size) to be considered.
Then produce an RFQ with: (1) A short intro — project, location, the trade you're soliciting. (2) 'Scope of work' — itemized, unambiguous, stating what's included down to the gray areas (cleanup, disposal, patching, who provides what). (3) 'Materials & responsibility' — supplied-by-GC vs supplied-by-sub. (4) 'Schedule' — needed start, duration, and any sequencing constraints. (5) 'What your quote must include' — a required line-item format, unit pricing where relevant, lead time, crew availability, and proof of insurance/license. (6) 'Exclusions & assumptions' to state up front. (7) 'How and by when to submit.' Then add a short, professional cover email.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the scope, dates, and specs I supplied; bracket anything missing rather than inventing requirements. Don't fabricate plan sheet numbers, standards, or quantities. Keep terms fair and clear so good subs want to bid. This solicits a quote; it is not a binding award or subcontract.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled RFQ sections, then a separate short 'Cover email to subcontractors.'

Title: Build a Realistic Project Schedule With the Right Sequence
Teaser: Turns your task list and constraints into a phased construction schedule with dependencies, milestones, inspection points, and buffer, so the sequence makes sense and the finish date is defensible.
PromptHint: List the tasks, trades, and any known durations or deadlines in [TASKS]. Add the start date, hard constraints, and crew/sub availability if known.
Prompt:
You are a scheduler who builds plans the crew can actually hit, because you respect dependencies, inspections, and the reality that drywall can't go up before the rough-in passes. The project:
<tasks>
[TASKS]
</tasks>
Start date and any hard deadlines: [START]
Constraints — crew/sub availability, weather, long-lead materials, access: [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: order the work by what must finish before the next thing can begin, mark the inspection or approval gates that block progress, and find the long-lead items that need ordering now. Identify the critical path — the chain that actually decides the finish date.
Then produce: (1) A phased schedule table — Phase/Task | Trade | Depends on | Duration | Target window — using only durations I gave and marking the rest '[estimate]'. (2) A 'Milestones & inspections' list with the gates that must pass before work continues. (3) A 'Long-lead / order now' list so nothing stalls the job. (4) A 'Critical path' note naming the tasks that drive the end date. (5) A 'Risks & buffer' line on where slippage is most likely and where you've added float. (6) A plain-English finish-date read with the assumptions it depends on.
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent durations, lead times, or inspection requirements — bracket what I didn't supply and label estimates clearly. Respect realistic trade sequencing; don't overlap tasks that physically can't run together. This is a planning schedule, not a guarantee — weather, approvals, and supply can shift dates.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The phased schedule table, then labeled 'Milestones & inspections,' 'Order now,' 'Critical path,' 'Risks & buffer,' and a one-line finish-date summary with its assumptions.

Title: Write the Tough Client Message About a Delay or Overrun
Teaser: Drafts the hard conversation — a delay, a cost overrun, or a found problem — as a calm, honest message that keeps the client's trust by pairing the bad news with a concrete plan.
PromptHint: Describe what happened, the cause, and the impact in [SITUATION]. Note how the client tends to react and what you're recommending.
Prompt:
You are a builder who can deliver bad news and keep the client, because you never hide a problem and you never bring one without a plan. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Client and how they tend to react (anxious, hot-tempered, detail-driven): [CLIENT]
Your recommended path forward: [RECOMMENDATION]
Think first, silently: separate the facts (what happened, why, the real impact on cost or schedule) from the emotion this client will feel. Decide the one clear recommendation and the options behind it. The goal is to be the calm, honest person in the room, not the defensive one.
Then write the message: (1) A direct, respectful opener that doesn't bury the lede or over-apologize. (2) 'What happened' — plain facts, owning what's yours without blame games. (3) 'What it means' — the honest impact on timeline and/or cost, using only figures I supplied. (4) 'How we're handling it' — your concrete plan and any options the client can choose between, with a clear recommendation. (5) 'What I need from you' — any decision and by when. (6) An offer to talk it through live and a steady, reassuring close. Then give me a 3-bullet phone version for if they'd rather hear it by call.
CONSTRAINTS: No defensiveness, no jargon shield, no fake reassurance. Don't fabricate causes, costs, or new dates — bracket what's unconfirmed. If fault is shared or unclear, say so honestly rather than assigning blame. This is professional communication, not a legal admission — keep it factual and aligned with the contract; suggest the client review terms if liability is in question.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A full message with subject line, then a 'Talking points for a call' list of three bullets.

Title: Turn Messy Field Notes Into a Daily Construction Log
Teaser: Cleans your rough end-of-day notes — crew, weather, work done, deliveries, issues, visitors — into a structured daily log that documents the job and protects you if anything is disputed later.
PromptHint: Dump your raw notes for the day into [NOTES] — crew, hours, weather, what got done, deliveries, problems, anyone on site. Add the project name and date.
Prompt:
You are a site supervisor whose daily logs have settled disputes years later, because they capture the facts cleanly while everyone's still on site. Today's raw notes:
<notes>
[NOTES]
</notes>
Project name and date: [PROJECT]
Think first, silently: a good daily log answers who was here, what conditions were, what got done, what arrived, and what went wrong — because those are the facts a delay claim, a back-charge, or a warranty fight turns on. Pull every one of those out of my notes and flag anything I should photograph or follow up.
Then produce a daily log with: (1) Header — project, date, weather/temperature, and a plain field-condition note (dry, muddy, frozen). (2) 'Crew & hours' — who, trade, and hours, from what I gave. (3) 'Work completed' — by area or trade, specific and factual. (4) 'Deliveries & equipment' — what arrived or was on site. (5) 'Issues, delays & directives' — problems, who was notified, any verbal instructions given (these matter most later). (6) 'Visitors & inspections' — anyone on site, any inspection result. (7) 'Follow-ups / photos needed' — open items and what to document. 
CONSTRAINTS: Record only what I reported — never invent hours, names, weather, or events; mark anything unclear as '[confirm]'. Keep it factual and neutral, not editorial. Note where a photo or written confirmation should back up a verbal directive. This is a project record; treat it as something that may be read in a dispute.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The structured daily log with all seven labeled sections, ending with a 'Follow-ups / photos needed' checklist.

Title: Write the Estimate Cover Letter That Closes the Sale
Teaser: Pairs your number with a short, trust-building cover note that frames the price as value, sets expectations, and gives the client an easy, confident way to say yes.
PromptHint: Paste the estimate summary, total, and what's included into [ESTIMATE]. Add what the client worried about and your scheduling/availability if known.
Prompt:
You are a contractor who knows the number on the estimate doesn't sell the job — the note attached to it does. The estimate:
<estimate>
[ESTIMATE]
</estimate>
What the client was worried about (price, mess, timing, a bad past contractor): [WORRY]
Your availability or start window, if known: [AVAILABILITY]
Think first, silently: the moment a client opens an estimate, they're scanning for reasons to trust or doubt. Decide how to frame the price as value, reassure the specific worry they have, and remove the friction from saying yes — without pressure or hype.
Then write a cover note: (1) A warm opening that thanks them and reflects back the project in their words. (2) 'What this includes' — a confident plain-language summary of the value behind the number, not a re-listing of the line items. (3) A line that directly eases their stated worry. (4) 'What happens next' — exactly how to accept, what a deposit secures, and a realistic start window. (5) An invitation to ask anything before deciding, and a steady, no-pressure close. Keep the whole note short enough to read in under a minute.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the price, scope, and dates I supplied; bracket anything missing. No false urgency, fake discounts, or guarantees you can't honor. Don't restate the full estimate — the attached estimate does that; the note sells the trust. Confident, human, brief.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A short cover note with a greeting and the labeled flow above, ending with a clear one-line 'How to move forward' and a signature placeholder.

Title: Reply to a Bad Review or Complaint Without Making It Worse
Teaser: Drafts a calm, professional response to an unhappy customer or online review that takes responsibility where fair, corrects the record where needed, and protects your reputation with future clients reading along.
PromptHint: Paste the complaint or review and what actually happened from your side into [COMPLAINT]. Note whether it's public, and what you're willing to offer.
Prompt:
You are a contractor who has learned that the audience for a complaint response isn't just the angry customer — it's every future client reading it. The complaint:
<complaint>
[COMPLAINT]
</complaint>
What actually happened from your side: [YOUR_SIDE]
Is it public (review site, social) or private, and what you're willing to offer: [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: figure out what's legitimately your fault, what's a misunderstanding, and what's simply unfair — because each needs different handling. The goal is to sound like the reasonable professional, never the defensive one, and never to win the argument at the cost of the audience's trust.
Then produce a response: (1) A calm, human opener that acknowledges their experience without groveling or admitting things that aren't true. (2) Where you're at fault — own it plainly and say what you're doing about it. (3) Where the facts differ — correct the record factually and without sarcasm, briefly. (4) A concrete path forward — what you're offering or proposing, and an invitation to resolve it directly (offline if it's public). (5) A short, professional close. Then give me a one-line tone note on what to avoid saying. If it's public, keep it tight; the offer to take it offline is the move.
CONSTRAINTS: Never be sarcastic, defensive, or insulting, even if the complaint is unfair. Don't admit fault you don't own, and don't invent facts — use only my account. Avoid disclosing private client details publicly. This is reputation communication, not legal advice — if the dispute involves money or liability, suggest handling specifics privately or with counsel.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The response (sized for public or private as noted), then a one-line 'Tone reminder' on what not to say.

Title: Plan a Phased Payment Schedule Tied to Real Milestones
Teaser: Designs a fair draw schedule that ties client payments to completed milestones, keeping your cash flow healthy and the client comfortable that they're paying for work actually done.
PromptHint: Paste the project scope, total contract price, and phases into [PROJECT]. Add any deposit norms, lender requirements, or local rules you know about.
Prompt:
You are a contractor who has been burned by front-loaded jobs and stiffed final payments, so you structure draws that keep cash flow steady while staying fair to the client. The project:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
Total contract price and major phases: [PRICE]
Deposit norms, lender draws, or rules you're aware of: [RULES]
Think first, silently: align each payment to a verifiable milestone so the client always sees value for money paid, while never letting your completed work get too far ahead of the cash collected. Decide a sensible deposit, progress draws tied to real completion points, and a retainage/final payment that motivates a clean finish.
Then produce: (1) A draw-schedule table — Milestone | What's complete at this point | % of total | Amount — using the total I supplied and marking any figure I didn't give as '[confirm]'. (2) A 'Deposit' line with what it secures (materials, scheduling) and why it's reasonable. (3) A 'Final payment / punch list' draw held until walkthrough sign-off, so completion stays motivated. (4) A plain-English 'How this protects both of us' note for the client. (5) A short list of what triggers each draw (inspection passed, phase signed off) to avoid disputes over 'is it done.'
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the price and phases I gave; bracket the rest and never invent amounts. Keep deposits and draws reasonable and tied to delivered value — no front-loading the client can't justify. Payment terms and retainage are governed by contract and vary by state/locality; flag that this is a planning template to confirm against local law and the signed agreement, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The draw-schedule table, then labeled 'Deposit,' 'Final payment,' a 'How this protects both of us' paragraph, and a 'Draw triggers' bullet list.
